title: Creative Commons Recognition and Copyright Infringement Perceptions Survey Experiment
text:
Introduction
This Online Appendix accompanies Some Respect Reserved: Survey Experiment Insights on the Perception of Creative Commons and Copyright Infringement, forthcoming in the Cardozo Arts & Entertainment Law Journal. It also documents the analysis in the dissertation chapter Hidden Costs of Open Licensing? A Survey Experiment on Creative Commons and Copyright Infringement in Creative Realism: Innovation Beyond the Ownership Incentive. The Author conveys his gratitude to the Journal’s editors and the dissertation committee members (Aaron Shaw, Aymar Jean Christian, and Matthew Kugler) and other scholars who have provided feedback on the Article and the underlying research. The Author has also benefited from presentations and discussion of this research with:
All errors and omissions are the Author’s alone.
The Appendix is organized as a reproducible Quarto Book. Every figure and table in the Article has a corresponding section here where the underlying data, code, and full statistical output are presented. Statistical results are generated directly from the underlying data. The code used to generate those results can be viewed in the source code of each chapter. The Appendix also includes detailed documentation of the survey instrument, data collection, and sample characteristics. The dataset and survey instruments are freely licensed for adaptation or replication.
The pre-registration for the survey experiment is available at the Open Science Foundation: https://osf.io/snxyt/. Some variations from the pre-registration are documented in the relevant sections of this Appendix, including the substitution of Group B1 for Group B in the Educational Use scenario analysis due to an error in data collection.

title: Creative Commons Recognition and Copyright Infringement Perceptions Survey Experiment
text:
0.1 The CAELJ Article

Thomas H. Rousse Some Respect Reserved: Survey Experiment Insights on the Perception of Creative Commons and Copyright Infringement. 44 Cardozo Arts & Entertainment Law Journal __ (forthcoming 2026).

The Article reports results from a pre-registered survey experiment (n = 1,239) examining the American public’s recognition of the Creative Commons brand and logo before being randomly split into treatment groups. Respondents were assigned to one of three treatment groups — CC Short (Group A), CC Full Text + Primer (Group B), and Copyright Marking Control (Group C) — and evaluated seven licensing scenarios. The study tests whether exposure to CC license terms affects perceptions of legal risk, preferred outcomes, and price estimates.

title: 5  Sample v. Population Analysis
text:
5.1 Overview
This Chapter compares the survey sample demographics against American Community Survey (ACS)1 benchmarks to assess external validity: How does the sample resemble the American adult population? The analysis covers five demographic characteristics: age, gender, race/ethnicity, education, and income.
ACS benchmarks loaded for variables: AGE, EDU, GENDER, HISPLAT, INCOME, RACE_ETH, RACE_ONLY 


Total ACS rows: 27

title: 5  Sample v. Population Analysis
text:
1 U.S. Census Bureau, 2023 1 Year Estimates, American Community Survey (ACS), https://www.census.gov/programs-surveys/acs.html.





Note



Study design note: The survey used a CloudResearch Connect2 panel. This service provides a quota-sampled convenience panel, not a probability sample. Like most online survey experiments, the study prioritizes internal validity (clean randomization between treatment groups) over representational sampling. The ACS comparison documents where the sample diverges from the general population, enabling readers to assess how findings might or might not generalize. In sum, CloudResearch’s “nationally representative” designation is a marketing term that does not reflect probability sampling or representativeness in the statistical sense.
Demographic divergences in this chapter fall into two distinct categories: (1) quota-controlled variables (age, gender, race, and Hispanic/Latino origin), where the sample follows CloudResearch Connect’s standard preset quotas, which depart from ACS proportions; and (2) uncontrolled variables (education and income), where no quota was set and the distribution reflects the organic composition of opt-in online survey participants.
Randomization balance (internal validity) is confirmed separately in Chapter 4. The analysis here uses Groups A, B, and C (n = 1,222).


2 Rachel Hartman, Aaron J. Moss, Shalom N. Jaffe, Cheskie Rosenzweig, Leib Litman & Jonathan Robinson, Introducing Connect by CloudResearch: Advancing Online Participant Recruitment in the Digital Age (2023), https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/ksgyr; CloudResearch, Connect for Researchers, https://www.cloudresearch.com/products/connect-for-researchers/.





Note



Reference population: ACS 2023 1-year estimates, U.S. civilian non-institutionalized population. Source tables: B01001 (sex by age), B03002 (Hispanic/Latino origin by race), B15003 (educational attainment, 25+), B19001 (household income). Data retrieved from data.gov via the tidycensus R package. See data/population/acs-source-notes.md for complete retrieval details and cell-level recoding decisions.

5.4 Race and Ethnicity
The survey asked Hispanic/Latino origin and race as separate questions, consistent with the Census Bureau approach. This section presents three complementary comparisons: (1) Hispanic/Latino origin alone, (2) racial identification alone, and (3) a combined race/ethnicity classification.

5.4.1 Hispanic/Latino Origin

Table 5.3: Hispanic/Latino origin: survey vs. ACS proportions.










Note



Quota note: The CloudResearch Connect standard panel preset, not the nationally representative ACS, set the Hispanic/Latino target at 8.3% (99.6 of 1,200 participants), compared to the ACS 2023 1-year estimate of approximately 19.4% for the U.S. adult population. CloudResearch’s preset applies equal 8.3% allocations across three non-White racial/ethnic groups (Black, Hispanic/Latino, and Other), with the remaining 83.3% allocated to White participants. This simplified stratification departs substantially from current Census proportions. The Author followed this preset without modification to facilitate cross-study comparability with other Connect panels, a design decision documented in the pre-registration (OSF: https://osf.io/snxyt/). This represents a deliberate methodological choice and a tradeoff of inexpensive survey participant pools against external validity, not a sampling error. The lack of representative racial and ethnic diversity is a well-known limitation of opt-in online survey platforms generally, which tend to underrepresent minorities and over-represent White respondents.3


3 See Courtney Kennedy, Andrew Mercer, Nick Hatley, and Scott Keeter, Evaluating Online Nonprobability Surveys, Pew Research Center (2016); Taylor C. Boas, Dino P. Chirsttenson, and David M. Glick, Recruiting Subjects to Survey Researchers: A Comparison of MTurk, Luc.id, and SurveyMonkey, 8 Pol. Sci. Res. & Methods 422, 427 (2020) (finding that MTurk-based samples significantly over-represent White respondents compared to Census benchmarks).

title: 5  Sample v. Population Analysis
text:
5.5 Education






Note



Measurement note: The ACS educational attainment table (B15003) covers the population aged 25 and older, while the survey targets adults 18 and older. The ACS proportions used here may over-represent higher education levels relative to the full 18+ population, since adults aged 18–24 are more likely to have not yet completed post-secondary education. No quota was set on educational attainment; the observed distribution reflects the organic composition of the CloudResearch Connect panel.
The heavy overrepresentation of college-educated respondents (70.5% Bachelor’s or higher vs. 35.7% ACS 25+) is a well-documented characteristic of opt-in online survey platforms generally.4


4 See Courtney Kennedy, Andrew Mercer, Nick Hatley, and Scott Keeter, Evaluating Online Nonprobability Surveys, Pew Research Center (2016); Taylor C. Boas, Dino P. Chirsttenson, and David M. Glick, Recruiting Subjects to Survey Researchers: A Comparison of MTurk, Luc.id, and SurveyMonkey, 8 Pol. Sci. Res. & Methods 422, 427 (2020) (finding that MTurk-based samples are significantly more educated than Census benchmarks).

Table 5.8: Population representativeness summary: chi-square goodness-of-fit tests for five demographic variables.










Note





Summary interpretation: Demographic divergences fall into two structurally distinct categories.
Quota-controlled variables (age, gender, race, Hispanic/Latino origin): divergences from the ACS reflect CloudResearch Connect’s standard panel preset, not sampling error. Gender is essentially aligned (h = 0.015) because the preset targets 50% female — nearly identical to the ACS. Age diverges modestly (h = 0.119, small) because the preset allocates 33.3% to the 45–59 cohort (vs. 23.2% ACS), producing the observed overrepresentation of that group; the observed age distribution tracks the preset quotas within 0.4 percentage points across all four bins. Race and Hispanic/Latino origin show medium divergences (HISPLAT h = 0.330; RACE_ETH h = 0.207; RACE_ONLY h = 0.296) that follow directly from the preset’s symmetric 83.3% White / 8.3% Black / 8.3% Hispanic / 8.3% Other structure — a heuristic that departs substantially from current Census proportions. These racial/ethnic divergences are documented in the pre-registration as a deliberate choice to follow CloudResearch’s standard preset for cross-study comparability (OSF: https://osf.io/snxyt/).
Uncontrolled variables (education and income): no quotas were set; distributions reflect the organic composition of the Connect panel. Education shows the largest divergence overall (h = 0.459, approaching large): the sample is heavily college-educated (70.5% Bachelor’s or higher vs. 35.7% ACS 25+), with HS-or-less respondents nearly absent (1.5% vs. 36.2%) — a well-documented characteristic of opt-in online survey panels. Note also that the ACS education benchmark covers adults 25+, while the survey targets 18+, modestly amplifying the apparent gap. Income diverges modestly (h = 0.152, small), with the sample concentrated in the $25k–$99.9k range and high earners underrepresented ($150k+: 9.5% vs. 21.5% ACS), consistent with lower participation rates among high-income individuals in paid online surveys.
The study’s primary claims rest on internal validity — clean randomization between treatment groups (confirmed in Chapter 4) — rather than representational sampling. These comparisons enable readers to assess which subpopulations are and are not well-represented and to calibrate the external generalizability of the findings accordingly.

2.1.2 The B1 Supplement
Group B1 is a supplemental data collection of 97 participants who replaced Group B for the Educational Use (EU) scenario. An error in the randomized distribution excluded B group participants from seeing the EU scenario. B1 participants completed only the EU portion of the survey; their non-EU scenario columns (PD, PU, CA, LS, WN, FS) are empty. B1 participants also did not complete the brand recognition battery (logo identification, NETSKILLS, BRANDSKILLS) or the demographic questions — these columns are NA for all B1 rows. Group B1 data differs from Group B in two ways: (1) B1 participants were recruited after the main data collection and may differ demographically from the original B group; (2) B1 participants were not exposed to the non-EU scenarios, which may have influenced their responses to the EU scenario. For these reasons, Group B1 is treated as a separate group in the EU scenario analysis (replacing the missing Group B data) and excluded from all other analyses.
For the EU scenario analysis (§7.3), the data is filtered to Groups A, B1, and C — Group B is excluded entirely. For all other scenarios, the data is filtered to Groups A, B, and C — Group B1 is excluded (since B1 participants did not answer those questions).

All data was collected in March 2024 via CloudResearch’s Connect platform, with the survey administered through Qualtrics.1

2.2 Experimental Design

2.2.1 Treatment Conditions
Respondents were randomly assigned by Qualtrics to one of three conditions:



Group
Condition

Each scenario presented a vignette describing a re-use of a creative work. The only difference between conditions was the licensing notice shown in the vignette and the presence of the CC primer for Group B. Control scenarios used a traditional “All Rights Reserved” copyright notice (except PD and WN, which have non-copyright control conditions). Treatment conditions replaced or supplemented this notice with a Creative Commons license designation — abbreviated in Treatment A, spelled out in full in Treatment B. Respondents in Treatment B also saw a one-page CC primer with educational information about the meaning of CC licenses and the specific permissions granted by each license type. 2.4 Response Scales

2.4.1 Q1: Likelihood of Legal Consequences (1–7 Likert)

“On a scale from 1–7, with 7 being the most likely: In your opinion, how likely is [the re-user] to face legal consequences for this use?”




Value
Label




1
Extremely unlikely


2
Moderately unlikely


3
Slightly unlikely


4
Neither likely nor unlikely


5
Slightly likely


6
Moderately likely


7
Extremely likely





2.4.2 Q2: Perceived Legal Consequences (1–7 Likert)

“On a scale from 1–7, with 7 being the most negative: In your opinion, if [the re-user] was sued for these actions, what legal consequences do you think they would face?”








Value
Label




1
No consequences


2
(unlabeled)


3
(unlabeled)


4

5
(unlabeled)


6
(unlabeled)


7
Very negative consequences, such as incarceration and/or a large fine





2.4.3 Q3: Preferred Legal Consequences (1–7 Likert)

“On a scale from 1–7, with 7 being the most negative: In your opinion, what legal consequences should [the re-user] face for these actions, if sued?”

The response scale is identical to Q2 (see above). Q2 asks what would happen; Q3 asks what should happen.


2.4.4 Delta (Q2 − Q3): Congruence Score
The Delta variable ([SCENARIO]25) is computed as Q2 minus Q3 for each scenario. It measures the gap between perceived law and preferred rule:

Positive Delta: Respondent believes consequences would be more severe than they should be
Zero: Perceived and preferred consequences are aligned
Negative Delta: Respondent believes consequences should be more severe than they would be

Range: −6 to +6 (integer).


2.4.5 Q4: Reasonable License Price (Continuous)

“Now imagine that before the use described in the scenario, [the re-user] and the owner of this work agreed on a price for a non-exclusive license — meaning that the owner can also license the work to others — that allows all of the use described in the scenario. In your opinion, what do you think would be a reasonable price for that license, if any? Please provide a single amount in US dollars.”


Type: Text entry (numeric)
Validation: Numeric values only, minimum $0, two decimal places
Unit: US dollars



2.4.6 NETSKILLS / BRANDSKILLS (1–5 Matrix)



Value
Label

This scale is used for both the Internet Skills battery (NETSKILLS) and the Brand Familiarity battery (BRANDSKILLS). See Brand Recognition & Internet Skills for item details.

2.7 Quality Control & Attention Checks
Three quality-control items were administered during the survey. These were used as screening questions — participants who failed attention/consent checks were excluded during data collection. The responses are not retained in the analysis dataset.

2.7.1 COMMIT (Commitment Question)

“We care about your opinions and we care about the quality of our survey data. To get the most accurate measures of your opinions, it is important that you provide thoughtful answers in good faith to each question in this survey. Please do not use text generated by AI or LLMs such as ChatGPT. Your answers to questions about your personal opinions will have no impact on your compensation. Do you commit to providing thoughtful answers in good faith to the questions in the survey?”




Value
Label




1
I can’t promise either way


2
Yes, I will


3
No, I will not





2.7.2 PINK (Color Attention Check)

“Which of the following colors is a lighter shade of red?”

Options: Green, Pink (correct), Yellow, Black, Blue.


2.7.3 FIRSTNAMEGEN (Name Attention Check)

“Which of the following names do you think is most likely to belong to someone who identifies as a female?”

Options: Henry, Robert, Elizabeth (correct), Timothy, Edward.


2.7.4 Feedback

CSV column: Feedback
Type: Optional free-text
Content: Open-ended participant feedback collected at the end of the survey

title: 7  Factorial Vignette Analysis Overview
text:
7.1 Analysis Structure
Each of the seven scenario chapters that follow uses a parallel structure:
Data: Groups A, B, C (n = 1,222) for all scenarios except Educational Use, which filters to Groups A, B1, C (n = 905) due to a supplemental data collection. See §3.1 (Codebook) for the B1 substitution.
Statistical methods are detailed in the Methods chapter. Effect sizes (VDA) are interpreted using the thresholds in Table 2.1.
Cross-reference format: figures are labeled @fig-[scenario]-q[N] (e.g., @fig-pd-q1); tables are labeled @tbl-[scenario]-q[N]-stats (e.g., @tbl-pd-q1-stats).

title: 7  Factorial Vignette Analysis Overview
text:
Scenario Context — the vignette presented to respondents
Q1: Legal Consequences — Kruskal-Wallis + Dunn’s post-hoc + VDA (figure and table)
Q2: What the Law Is — Kruskal-Wallis + Dunn’s + VDA
Q3: What the Law Should Be — Kruskal-Wallis + Dunn’s + VDA
Δ (Delta, Q2 − Q3) — within-group Wilcoxon signed-rank tests
Q4: Price estimate — Kruskal-Wallis + Dunn’s

title: 7  Scenario Analyses Overview
text:
7.3 Cross-Scenario Summary Tables
The following tables consolidate the results from all seven scenarios for each question type (Q1, Q2, Q3, and Delta). Each cell shows Mean (SD) by group. Treatment groups additionally show the percentage difference from control in brackets. For the Delta tables, brackets show the mean delta as a percentage of the group’s Q2 mean — the fraction of perceived consequences that exceeds respondent preferences.
The VDA (Vargha & Delaney’s A) tables show pairwise effect sizes with significance markers from Dunn’s post-hoc tests (Holm-adjusted). Effect size thresholds follow Vargha & Delaney (2000): (S)mall \(\geq\) .56, (M)edium \(\geq\) .64, (L)arge \(\geq\) .71.






NoteEU Scenario



Rows marked with a dagger (†) use supplemental Group B1 (n = 97) in place of Group B. See §3.1 for the B1 substitution.

title: 7  Factorial Vignette Analysis Overview
text:
7.2 Analysis Pipeline
Every scenario chapter (§§7.1–7.7) calls a single function, analyze_full_scenario(), and receives back a complete set of plots, descriptive statistics, and test results. That one call sets in motion a coordinated pipeline of seven R modules, each responsible for a distinct layer of the analysis. This section documents those modules: what each one does, why it was designed the way it was, and how the pieces fit together.
All seven files live in analysis/functions/ and are sourced at book startup by _common.R. The source code for each module is folded below its description; click Show source to expand it. Code results are cached for HTML display but can be manually run as an R or Positron Notebook.

7.2.1 Pipeline Overview






flowchart TD
  CSV["rousse-cc-survey-2024.csv\n(1,319 obs, all groups)"]

  subgraph common ["_common.R  (book startup)"]
    CSV --> dp["data_prep.R\nload_survey_data()\ncreate_all_deltas()"]
    dp --> sdA["survey_data\nGROUP ∈ {A, B, C}\nn = 1,222"]
    dp --> sdB["survey_data_eu\nGROUP ∈ {A, B1, C}\nn = 905"]
  end

  subgraph chapter ["Per-scenario chapter  (§7.1 – §7.7)"]
    sdA --> ma["master_analysis.R\nanalyze_full_scenario()"]
    sdB --> ma
    ma --> af["analysis_functions.R\nanalyze_scenario_question()\ncreate_scenario_plot()\ncreate_delta_plot()\ncreate_price_plot()"]
    af --> sh["stats_helpers.R\nvalidate_group_data()\nKruskal-Wallis · Dunn's post-hoc\nVDA effect sizes\nWilcoxon signed-rank (delta)"]
    af --> pt["plotting_themes.R\nget_standard_theme()\nget_standard_plot_args()\nget_price_plot_args()"]
    af --> ex["export_helpers.R\nexport_plot_to_tiff()\ndata / figures / v2 /"]
    ma --> out["Returns nested list:\n$plots · $statistics · $tests"]
  end

  rc["randomization_checks.R\ncheck_continuous_covariate()\ncheck_categorical_covariate()\ncheck_brand_recognition()"] -.->|"Ch. 4–5 only"| sdA




Figure 7.1: Analysis pipeline: from raw CSV to scenario chapter outputs.





The pipeline separates cleanly into three tiers: data (Module 1), statistics and visualization (Modules 2–5), and orchestration (Module 7). Module 6 supports only Chapters 4 and 5 and is never called by the scenario analysis chain.


7.2.2 Module 1: data_prep.R — Data Ingestion
data_prep.R handles everything that must happen before any statistics can be run: loading the CSV, constructing computed columns, and producing the two analysis views that the rest of the book uses.
The central design decision is single-file architecture. The two analysis views are created in _common.R by filtering on GROUP: survey_data keeps Groups A, B, and C (n = 1,222); survey_data_eu keeps Groups A, B1, and C (n = 905). The prepare_eu_data() function in this module handles the original two-file workflow and is preserved for provenance, but it is not called at book startup.
The other key contribution is delta variable creation. Each scenario includes a question about what the law currently permits (Q2) and a question about what respondents prefer (Q3). Their difference (Δ = Q2 − Q3) is stored in a column named [SCENARIO]25 (e.g., PD25). A positive delta means the law is stricter than respondents prefer; a negative delta means the law is more permissive. create_all_deltas() loops over all seven scenario codes and creates these columns in a single pass, and it gracefully skips any scenario whose Q2/Q3 columns are absent.

7.2.3 Module 2: stats_helpers.R — Statistical Calculations
stats_helpers.R provides the statistical layer: validation, descriptive summaries, hypothesis tests, and effect sizes. Its design philosophy is to keep statistical decisions in one place so that all seven scenario chapters are guaranteed to use identical methods.
The non-parametric choice is deliberate. The outcome variables are 7-point Likert scales: ordinal, bounded, and typically non-normal in distribution. Kruskal-Wallis is the appropriate omnibus test, and Dunn’s test (via rstatix::dunn_test(), Holm correction) is the appropriate post-hoc. Pairwise Wilcoxon was considered but rejected: Dunn’s test is specifically designed for post-hoc comparisons following a Kruskal-Wallis test and uses rank-sum scores from the full dataset, which gives it more appropriate statistical properties in this context.






NoteDesign note: Why Vargha & Delaney’s A over Cohen’s d or r?





Effect sizes based on means (Cohen’s d) assume interval-level data. For ordinal scales, Vargha & Delaney’s A is the recommended alternative: it is a rank-based probability that a randomly selected observation from Group X exceeds a randomly selected observation from Group Y. A value of 0.50 indicates no difference; values above 0.50 indicate Group 1 dominance, values below 0.50 indicate Group 2 dominance. Thresholds used throughout this appendix follow Vargha & Delaney (2000) and are given in Table 1.1.



The delta analysis uses a different test: test_delta_nonzero() runs a one-sample Wilcoxon signed-rank test for each group separately, testing whether that group’s median delta is significantly different from zero. A non-zero median delta indicates a gap between respondents’ perception of current law and their normative preference.

7.2.4 Module 3: plotting_themes.R — Visual Consistency
plotting_themes.R is the style guide for every figure in this Appendix. By centralizing all ggplot2 theme and argument settings, the module guarantees that every plot across all seven scenario chapters shares the same fonts, sizes, color choices, significance bracket styling, and central tendency markers.
The base theme is Tufte (via ggthemes::theme_tufte()), following Edward Tufte’s principle of maximizing the data-to-ink ratio. Unnecessary gridlines and borders are removed; the statistical signal is left to speak for itself. Font resolution is handled through the PLOT_FONT global variable, which is set in _common.R before this module is sourced; all functions default to base_family = PLOT_FONT so that the book-level font choice propagates everywhere automatically.
Two argument-list functions address the structural difference between Likert-scale questions and the price question.
get_standard_plot_args() configures the standard Q1/Q2/Q3/delta plots: no individual points shown, means displayed (parametric centrality), and significance brackets limited to statistically significant pairs.
get_price_plot_args() modifies the recipe for price data: individual estimates are shown as semi-transparent jittered dots, and the central tendency switches to median (50th percentile) because price distributions are right-skewed and means are not robust to the outliers that remain after the 99th-percentile trim.

7.2.5 Module 4: analysis_functions.R — Per-Question Plots
analysis_functions.R is where statistical tests and visualizations are actually assembled. Its four functions correspond to the four structural plot types that appear across the scenario chapters: standard questions (Q1/Q2/Q3), delta questions (Δ = Q2 − Q3), price questions (Q4), and a master dispatcher that routes each question to the appropriate specialized function.
The core plot engine throughout is ggstatsplot::ggbetweenstats(). This function was chosen because it combines group-level box plots, individual jitter (optional), pairwise significance brackets, and statistical test results in a single, opinionated call. The tradeoff is that the function’s defaults are opinionated and occasionally need to be overridden, leading to plotting_themes.R as a separate layer.
A critical detail shared by all three plot functions is group factor reordering. The raw GROUP column contains the strings "A", "B", "C", and (for EU) "B1". Left to its default, R would display these alphabetically. Each function refactors the column to display order C → A → B (Control first, then treatments in ascending information density), and maps the codes to human-readable labels: "Control (C)", "Short (A)", "Full + Primer (B)", and "Full + Primer (B1)". This ordering is applied consistently before any statistical test is run, so all group comparisons and labels in the output match this canonical ordering.
create_delta_plot() adds one step beyond the standard plot: after producing the between-group visualization, it runs a one-sample Wilcoxon signed-rank test for each group separately. The test result finding whether each group’s median delta is significantly different from zero is returned alongside the plot and appears in the scenario chapters as the Wilcoxon results table.
create_price_plot() adds outlier removal via remove_outliers_by_group() before any statistics are run. Price estimates are right-skewed and occasionally extreme; removing the top 1% within each group independently (rather than across the full sample) prevents any one group’s outliers from distorting the visual scale.

7.2.6 Module 5: export_helpers.R — Figure Export
export_helpers.R handles the mechanics of saving analysis outputs to disk. export_plot_to_tiff() is the function called by analyze_scenario_question() whenever export_tiff = TRUE. It uses R’s base graphics device system—tiff() → print(plot) → dev.off()—to write a 300 DPI TIFF suitable for journal submission.
The default output path uses here::here("data", "figures", "v2"), which resolves relative to the project root regardless of the working directory at the time of rendering. The filename convention is [SCENARIO][QUESTION][WIDTH×10].tiff — for example, PD165.tiff for the Public Domain Q1 plot at 6.5 inches wide. The generate_filename() function encodes this convention explicitly.
The remaining functions (export_plots_batch(), save_analysis_results(), export_stats_to_csv(), export_all_results()) support the standalone batch workflow and the generate_full_report() orchestrator. They are not called during normal book rendering.

7.2.7 Module 6: randomization_checks.R — Covariate Balance
randomization_checks.R is deliberately isolated from the scenario analysis chain. Its functions appear in Chapter 4 (?sec-randomization) and Chapter 5 (Section 2.6), and they are never called by analyze_full_scenario(). Keeping balance checks in their own module prevents the scenario chapters from accumulating diagnostic overhead, and it makes the covariate-balance logic easy to find and audit independently.
check_continuous_covariate() handles numeric variables (Age, Income, Education). The test selection is adaptive: Age and Income are tested with ANOVA; scale-based variables (Internet skills) use Kruskal-Wallis. check_categorical_covariate() handles nominal variables (Race, Gender) via chi-square test. The $proportions component gives within-group proportions, which are more interpretable for balance assessment than raw counts.
The module also contains check_brand_recognition(), which analyzes the four-item brand recognition battery (Creative Commons, Byteyield, Microsoft, Mozilla). Byteyield is a fictitious brand included as an attention check; its recognition mean should be well below the 2.5 threshold.






NoteWhy isolate randomization checks from scenario analysis?





If randomization_checks.R were sourced inside each scenario chapter, every chapter would re-run the balance tests — repeating the same checks seven times with no additional information. Placing the checks in dedicated chapters (4 and 5) and in a standalone module makes the one-time nature of the diagnostic explicit.

7.2.8 Module 7: master_analysis.R — Orchestration
master_analysis.R is the top of the call stack. Its central function, analyze_full_scenario(), is what each scenario chapter calls. It takes a scenario code (e.g., "PD"), a full scenario name (e.g., "Public Domain"), the data, and an export_tiffs flag, then runs all five question types (Q1, Q2, Q3, Delta, Price) through the pipeline and collects the results into a named list.
The module’s hierarchy is explicit in the source comments: generate_full_report() → analyze_all_scenarios() → analyze_full_scenario() → analyze_scenario_question() → specialist plot functions. The Quarto book uses only the third level—analyze_full_scenario()—because each chapter handles one scenario and the book structure itself provides the “all scenarios” organization.
The EU scenario receives special handling: if scenario_code == "EU" and a data_b1 argument is provided, the function swaps in the EU-specific dataset. In the Quarto book, _common.R pre-computes both survey_data and survey_data_eu, so each scenario chapter passes the correct dataset directly.
Return structure:
results
├── $plots        — ggplot objects: $q1, $q2, $q3, $delta, $price
├── $statistics   — data frames (mean, SD, median by group): $q1 … $price
├── $tests        — $delta: list of wilcox.test() per group
│                   $price: outlier-trimmed data frame
├── $summary      — long-format table combining all $statistics entries
├── $scenario_code
└── $scenario_name

7.2.9 Call-Site Pattern
With all seven modules loaded, every scenario chapter reduces to a single call:
The EU scenario passes survey_data_eu (Groups A/B1/C, n = 905) instead of survey_data. The pipeline’s modularity means that changing any single layer, replacing the theme in plotting_themes.R, adding an effect size in stats_helpers.R, or switching export format in export_helpers.R propagates automatically to all seven scenario chapters without touching the chapter files themselves.

Figure 2: Public Domain — Q2: Perceived legal consequences under current law by treatment group.





Q2 shifts from likelihood to severity: if the executive’s company were sued, what legal consequences do respondents believe would result under the current law? The response scale runs from 1 (“No consequences”) to 7 (“Very negative consequences, such as incarceration and/or a large fine”), with 4 anchored at “Some negative consequences, such as a court order to stop and/or a moderate fine.”
The C > A > B pattern holds with even stronger effect. As reported in Table 3, the Control mean is 2.78 (SD = 1.70), Treatment A is 2.37 (SD = 1.77), and Treatment B is 1.40 (SD = 1.00). The most explicit explanation of the work’s copyright status produces the lowest estimate of legal consequences: Treatment B’s mean is approximately 50% lower than the Control. The Kruskal-Wallis test returns a large effect size, and all pairwise comparisons are significant (p < 0.001). Pairwise VDA effect sizes are reported in Table 4.
Treatment B’s violin plot in Figure 2 is particularly instructive: the wide base at the bottom of the scale and few selections above it make clear that respondents recognized re-using the public domain work, even for commercial purposes, is not infringement. The explicit statement that a work is “free of known copyright restrictions” collapses uncertainty. Treatment B’s standard deviation (1.00) is roughly 40% smaller than the Control’s (1.70), indicating not just a lower mean but substantially greater consensus among respondents.

Q3 uses the same severity scale as Q2 but asks what consequences respondents think should follow, measuring normative preferences rather than perceptions of current law. For the Public Domain scenario, where the use is non-infringing under all conditions, Q3 responses should closely mirror Q2: respondents who believe there are no legal consequences under current law should also believe there should be none.
The results confirm this pattern. Treatment B again anchors the bottom of the scale (M = 1.38, SD = 1.02), approximately 47% lower than the Control (M = 2.60, SD = 1.67). Treatment A falls between (M = 2.21, SD = 1.70), approximately 15% lower than the Control. The ordering C > A > B is maintained, and the means track Q2 closely — explaining that a work is free from copyright restrictions leads respondents to estimate lesser punishment to a moderate degree for Treatment A and to a moderate-to-large degree for Treatment B. The pairwise effect sizes mirror Q2: all comparisons are significant (p < 0.001), with VDA values indicating a consistent treatment gradient (see Table 6).
The close correspondence between Q2 and Q3 is itself a finding: for the Public Domain scenario, respondents’ perceptions of current law align with their normative preferences. This is exactly what one would expect for a baseline scenario where the use does not infringe. There is no tension between “what the law says” and “what respondents think the law should say.”

title: 7.1 Public Domain
text:
7.1.6 Summary






TipKey Findings — Public Domain



The Public Domain scenario serves as the study’s baseline. Because the Blue Marble photograph is a U.S. government work not subject to copyright (17 U.S.C. § 105), the depicted use is non-infringing across all treatment conditions. The key findings are:

Baseline partially confirmed. All group means fall in the low range of the 7-point scale (1.38–2.95 for Q1–Q3), confirming that respondents generally perceive low legal risk for public domain use. However, significant between-group differences exist across all ordinal questions. The Public Domain scenario is not as “flat” as a pure baseline would predict.
C > A > B pattern confirmed. The pre-registered ordering holds consistently across Q1, Q2, Q3, and price. Control respondents perceive the highest risk and estimate the highest license prices; Treatment B respondents perceive the lowest risk and estimate the lowest prices.
Treatment B produces near-consensus. The explicit statement that the work is “free of known copyright restrictions” drives Treatment B means to approximately 1.40 on the 7-point scale with standard deviations near 1.00. The narrow distributions in the violin plots indicate strong respondent agreement, not just lower means.
Law–preference gap is zero. Delta (Q2 - Q3) is near zero across all groups, and the between-group Kruskal-Wallis test is not significant. This confirms that the Public Domain scenario functions as intended: respondents’ perceptions of current law match their normative preferences. There is no systematic tension between “what the law says” and “what it should say.”
Residual confusion persists. Despite non-infringing use, Control and Treatment A respondents still report moderate perceived risk (means of 2.50–2.95), well above the scale minimum of 1. This suggests incomplete public understanding of public domain status. Many respondents in these groups do not fully appreciate that U.S. government works carry no copyright restrictions.
Framing matters even for clear cases. The central implication is that how copyright information is presented materially affects risk perception, even when the underlying legal question is straightforward. For a non-infringing baseline, moving from “U.S. Government Work” to an explicit statement of copyright-free status reduces perceived risk by approximately 50% and license price estimates by roughly two-thirds.

Figure 2





Q2 asks respondents to set aside the likelihood question and consider what the law currently provides — conditional on a lawsuit being filed. Filesharing occupies a distinctive cultural position here. The recording industry’s enforcement campaigns of the 2000s were widely publicized, with statutory damages of up to $150,000 per work for willful infringement making national headlines. The question is whether CC licensing shifts respondents’ estimates of the severity of consequences, even in conditions where the use is non-infringing.
The Control group estimated consequences at 4.51 (SD = 1.49), Treatment A at 3.96 (SD = 1.73), and Treatment B at 3.08 (SD = 1.86). The Kruskal-Wallis test was significant (\(\chi^2\)(2) = 119.91, p = < .001). The recording industry’s enforcement campaigns left a lasting imprint: even in the treatment conditions — where the redistribution is authorized by the CC BY 4.0 license — respondents still anticipated meaningful consequences. The question is not whether respondents perceive filesharing as legally risky (they do), but whether CC licensing information moderates the perceived severity. See Figure 2 and Table 3 for distributions and descriptive statistics, and Table 4 for pairwise comparisons and effect sizes.

Q3 shifts from descriptive to normative — not what the law is, but what it should be. For filesharing, this is particularly interesting because the control describes an infringing use (unauthorized distribution under standard copyright) while the treatments describe a non-infringing use (redistribution authorized by CC BY 4.0). If respondents internalize the licensing information, the treatment groups should prefer substantially less severe consequences than the control. The gap between Q3 and Q2 (see 7.7.4 Delta (Q2 - Q3): Filesharing Regulation Gap) then reveals whether respondents believe existing law is calibrated to their preferences.
The results in Table 5 reveal a striking pattern. The Control group preferred consequences at 4.15 (SD = 1.58), Treatment A at 4.09 (SD = 2.02), and Treatment B at 2.88 (SD = 1.84). The Kruskal-Wallis test was significant (\(\chi^2\)(2) = 112.30, p = < .001). The full-text licensing condition with the Creative Commons primer (Treatment B) makes a notable difference in respondents’ normative preferences, with a substantial decrease in preferred legal consequences from the control. The abbreviated licensing condition without education (Treatment A), on the other hand, is indistinguishable from the copyright control — despite the fact that Treatment A describes a non-infringing use and the control describes an infringing one. Comparing the broad base of Treatment B to Treatment A in the violin plots (Figure 3), it is clear that a much larger portion of Treatment B respondents recognized that the re-use was permitted by the license. Pairwise VDA effect sizes are reported in Table 6.

The question is what respondents believe a non-exclusive license for this redistribution would cost. Note that price estimates are right-skewed and top 1% trimmed (see Table 9).
After trimming the top 1% of responses, median license price estimates were $1,500 for the Control, $1,000 for Treatment A, and $300 for Treatment B. The gap between means and medians in Table 9 reflects extreme right-tail outliers: some respondents estimated license values in the hundreds of thousands, pulling means far above the medians. Despite this noise, the consistent ordering across groups — with the control highest and Treatment B lowest — confirms the general CC pricing discount pattern observed across the study’s scenarios. title: 7.7 Filesharing
text:
7.7.6 Summary






TipKey Findings — Filesharing



Filesharing is a Non-infringing Treatments, Infringing Control scenario: the student’s redistribution of CC-licensed music with proper attribution is within the license terms for Treatments A and B, while the control describes unauthorized distribution under standard copyright. This scenario tests whether CC licensing can overcome the powerful cultural association between filesharing and copyright infringement.

Divergent legal outcomes obscured by cultural association. The treatment conditions describe non-infringing uses (redistribution authorized by CC BY 4.0 with attribution), while the control describes classic copyright infringement. Yet respondents across all groups perceived substantial legal risk. The deeply ingrained public association between filesharing and piracy — forged by the recording industry’s mass-litigation campaigns of the 2000s — overrides careful legal analysis of the licensing terms.
Treatment B succeeds where Treatment A fails. Treatment B (full-text license + educational primer) produces meaningful reductions in perceived risk, perceived consequences, and normative preferences relative to the control. Treatment A (abbreviated CC symbol), however, is often statistically indistinguishable from the control — despite describing a non-infringing use. This is one of the starkest demonstrations in the study of the CC symbol’s insufficiency without accompanying education.
CC symbol alone cannot overcome infringement framing. On Q3 (normative preferences), Treatment A respondents prefer consequences at nearly the same level as Control respondents, even though Treatment A describes a lawful use and the Control describes an unlawful one. The CC BY symbol, without the educational primer explaining what the license permits, fails to shift respondents’ moral intuitions about filesharing. The cultural frame dominates the legal frame.
Unusual Delta pattern reveals Treatment A ambiguity. The Control and Treatment B both show positive deltas (respondents prefer more lenient treatment than they believe current law provides), consistent with a general sense that filesharing penalties are overly punitive. Treatment A, however, shows a negative delta — respondents want more severe consequences than they believe current law provides. This anomaly likely reflects Treatment A’s ambiguous signaling: enough information to register that some authorization exists, but not enough to understand what is actually permitted.
Pricing discount pattern confirmed despite noise. License price estimates follow the consistent CC pricing discount (Control highest, Treatment B lowest), though large standard deviations and extreme right-tail outliers reflect the genuine difficulty of estimating the economic value of mass music redistribution rights. The gap between means and medians is among the largest across scenarios.
Education is the critical variable. Across all five measures, the pattern is consistent: Treatment B’s educational primer is what drives the treatment effect. Without it, the CC symbol alone is insufficient to communicate that filesharing under a CC BY license is lawful redistribution, not piracy. For a use context this deeply associated with infringement, education is not merely helpful — it is essential.

title: 7.4 Commercial Advertising
text:
7.4.6 Summary






TipKey Findings — Commercial Advertising



The Commercial Advertising scenario is the study’s clearest case of uniform infringement: the CC BY-NC 4.0 license explicitly prohibits commercial use, so the bike shop’s Facebook advertisement infringes under all three conditions. Any between-group differences reflect how CC licensing information shapes perceptions of an act whose legal status does not change. The key findings are:

Uniformly infringing scenario confirmed. All group means for Q1–Q3 sit in the moderate-to-high range of the 7-point scale, confirming that respondents across all conditions recognize commercial advertising use of a copyrighted photograph as carrying meaningful legal risk. The high baseline in the Control group (Q1 M = 3.25, Q2 M = 3.15) indicates that respondents associate high-visibility commercial use with legal exposure regardless of licensing information.
CC signaling, not legal change. Unlike the Public Domain scenario (where treatment information clarifies the absence of copyright) or Filesharing (where the license may alter the infringement analysis), the Commercial Advertising scenario isolates the signaling function of CC licensing. Treatment effects here cannot be attributed to changes in legal status — the NonCommercial clause does not excuse commercial use. Any observed differences reflect how the presence and detail of licensing information shapes respondent perceptions.
NonCommercial clause paradox. The CC BY-NC license’s explicit NonCommercial restriction creates a potential paradox: rather than reducing perceived risk (as CC information does in non-infringing scenarios), the restriction may increase perceived risk by signaling that the licensor specifically anticipated and prohibited commercial use. The significant between-group difference on Q1 (Kruskal-Wallis p = < .001) is consistent with this signaling mechanism.
Pricing patterns reflect commercial context. License price estimates (median: Control = $100, Treatment A = $100, Treatment B = $100) are calibrated to the commercial advertising context, with the right-skewed distribution reflecting respondents who anchored on the high-visibility, high-impression nature of the use.
Law–preference gap direction. All three groups’ Delta values were positive, indicating that respondents across all conditions perceived current law as imposing stricter consequences for commercial advertising infringement than they personally preferred. This suggests that even for a clearly infringing commercial use, respondents’ normative preferences are somewhat more lenient than their perceptions of what the law currently requires. Within-group Wilcoxon tests confirm this gap is significant for at least one treatment condition.
Normative stability. Q3 means were relatively stable across conditions compared to Q2, suggesting that CC licensing information primarily shapes perceptions of the legal status quo rather than underlying moral intuitions about the appropriate severity of consequences for commercial infringement.

title: 7.2 Personal Use
text:
7.2.6 Summary






TipKey Findings — Personal Use



Personal Use is a de minimis infringement scenario: the re-use is private, non-commercial, and limited to a single copy displayed in the home. The use infringes across all conditions (Control: unauthorized reproduction; Treatments: CC BY violation due to missing attribution). This scenario was designed to produce floor-level responses across the board, and it does.

De minimis floor effect dominates. All group means cluster near the bottom of the 7-point scale across Q1–Q3 (Control: 1.56–2.00; Treatment B: 1.40–1.61), confirming that respondents intuitively perceive private, non-commercial use as unlikely to draw legal consequences regardless of licensing condition. Notably, Personal Use scores lower than the Public Domain baseline, consistent with the de minimis character of the use rather than its technical legality.
CC signaling extends beyond legal content. The treatment conditions do not change the legal analysis — the use infringes under both standard copyright and CC BY 4.0. Yet a detectable treatment effect emerges on normative preferences (Q3) and pricing (Q4). This suggests that CC licensing carries a signaling function beyond its legal content: it shifts respondents’ sense of what consequences are appropriate and what the use is worth, even when the use remains technically infringing.
Normative preference shift on Q3. While all means are near the floor, the Control group prefers slightly more consequences than either treatment group (Control M = 1.83 vs. Treatment B M = 1.46). The Kruskal-Wallis test for Q3 was significant (p = < .001). CC licensing shifts respondents’ sense of the appropriate level of punishment, not just their estimate of its likelihood.
Negative Delta across all groups. All group deltas are negative — Control (M = 0.17), Treatment A (M = 0.20), Treatment B (M = 0.15) — indicating respondents believe consequences should be less severe than what current law provides. All within-group Wilcoxon signed-rank tests were significant, confirming that each group’s delta differs from zero. Treatment B shows the smallest gap, consistent with the full-text license compressing both Q2 and Q3 toward the floor.
Pricing discount confirms CC signal. Median price estimates — Control ($25), Treatment A ($15), Treatment B ($19) — are modest across the board, reflecting the limited economic value of private use. Both treatment groups estimate lower prices than the Control, consistent with a general CC discount effect even for infringing use.
Design expectations confirmed. Where the CC license does not change the legal analysis, its informational effect on perceived risk (Q1, Q2) is minimal. The effects that do appear — on normative preferences and pricing — isolate the non-legal signaling dimension of Creative Commons licensing.

title: 1  Experimental Design & Methods
text:
1.1 Overview
This Appendix provides the full methodological detail, statistical output, and reproducible code necessary for independent evaluation and replication of the results.
Every figure and table in this Appendix is generated from the canonical dataset using the embedded R code, which can be inspected by expanding the “Show Code” blocks. An explanation of the analysis is included in Chapter 7. This repository includes literate programming code for all analyses, enabling independent reproduction of the results. The code is organized into modular R scripts in the R/ directory, which are sourced by the R Markdown files for each chapter. The data processing and analysis workflow is orchestrated by the prepare_all_data() function in R/prepare_data.R, which is called at book startup to load and prepare all datasets. The data files are stored in the data/ directory, with the raw survey data available on OSF.io.

title: 1  Experimental Design & Methods
section: 1.2 Creative Commons Brand and Logo Recognition
text:
1.2 Creative Commons Brand and Logo Recognition
All respondents were asked their familiarity with several logos, including the Creative Commons logo. Respondents who identified the Creative Commons logo were asked to explain in free text what they thought it represented. Responses were manually reviewed by the author. All respondents were also asked to identify their familiarity with several brands, including Creative Commons. This analysis establishes baseline familiarity with the CC brand and logo, which is relevant for interpreting the treatment effects in the vignette analysis.

title: 1  Experimental Design & Methods
text:
1.3 Factorial Vignette Hypotheses
The study tests whether exposure to Creative Commons (CC) license information affects how members of the American public perceive copyright infringement across a range of scenarios. Participants were randomly assigned to one of three groups:

Group C (Control): Standard copyright notice (or government work / Wikipedia link, depending on scenario)
Group A (CC Succinct): Creative Commons license symbol with minimal text
Group B (CC Verbose): Creative Commons license symbol with extended explanatory text

The central hypothesis, pre-registered prior to data collection, is that the likelihood and severity of perceived legal consequences under the current law will follow the ordering:

C > A > B

That is, respondents exposed to a traditional copyright notice (Group C) will perceive the highest legal risk, those shown a succinct CC license (Group A) will perceive intermediate risk, and those given verbose CC license information (Group B) will perceive the lowest risk. This ordering reflects the prediction that CC license exposure reduces perceived legal consequences, and that more detailed license information produces a stronger effect.
This prediction applies most directly to scenarios where the CC license materially changes the legal analysis — for example, where Control respondents see an infringing scenario but Treatment respondents see a non-infringing one (Educational Use, Filesharing). For universally infringing scenarios (Personal Use, Commercial Advertising, Large Scale Copying), the expected treatment effect is smaller, as the CC license does not eliminate legal risk for those uses.

1.4.3 Survey Instrument
For each scenario vignette, respondents answered four questions:
Q1 — Likelihood of legal consequences (7-point Likert, 1 = “Extremely unlikely” to 7 = “Extremely likely”):

“In your opinion, how likely is [the re-user] to face legal consequences for this use?”

Q2 — Perceived legal consequences under current law (7-point scale, 1 = “No consequences,” 4 = “Some negative consequences, such as a court order to stop and/or a moderate fine,” 7 = “Very negative consequences, such as incarceration and/or a large fine”):

“In your opinion, if [the re-user] was sued for these actions, what legal consequences do you think they would face?”

Q3 — Normative preferences (same 7-point scale as Q2):

“In your opinion, what legal consequences should [the re-user] face for these actions, if sued?”

Q4 — License price estimate (continuous, USD):

“Now imagine that before the use(s) described in the scenario, [the re-user] and the owner of this work agreed on a price for a non-exclusive license […]. In your opinion, what do you think would be a reasonable price for that license, if any?”

The complete survey instrument, including vignette text and all response options, is available at data/survey/CCMasterv5-24-02-12.pdf.


1.4.4 Participant Recruitment
Participants (N = 1,200 target, evenly divided across three groups) were recruited through CloudResearch Connect, an online survey platform. The study used CloudResearch’s preset nationally representative quotas:

title: 1  Experimental Design & Methods
text:
1.5 Statistical Methods

1.5.1 Rationale for Non-Parametric Tests
The outcome variables for likelihood of legal consequences (Q1), predicted severity of legal consequences (Q2), and preferred severity of legal consequences (Q3) are ordinal 7-point Likert scales. Treating ordinal data as interval-scale would impose unwarranted assumptions about equal spacing between response categories. Because the expected answers often turn on whether or not a respondent grasps a legal rule and correctly determines whether the scenario infringes, distributions frequently violate assumptions of normality. Similarly, results are often heavily skewed given that some scenarios present an infringing control scenario and non-infringing treatments. Non-parametric statistics are applied throughout, reducing the power of the statistical analysis but ensuring robustness.

VDA > 0.50 indicates Group 1 tends to score higher than Group 2; VDA < 0.50 indicates the reverse.


1.5.4 Delta (Q2 – Q3)
Delta (notation: \(\Delta\)) represents the gap between a respondent’s perception of what the law is (Q2) and what it should be (Q3):
\[\Delta = Q2 - Q3\]

Positive Delta: The respondent perceives the law as stricter than their normative preference — they believe current legal consequences exceed what they think is appropriate.
Negative Delta: The respondent perceives the law as more lenient than preferred — they want stricter consequences than they believe the law currently provides.
Zero Delta: Perfect alignment between perceived law and normative preference.

Delta is computed for each respondent within each scenario. The between-group analysis uses Kruskal-Wallis and Dunn’s tests (same as Q1–Q3). Additionally, a Wilcoxon signed-rank test is applied within each group to test whether the group’s median Delta differs significantly from zero — that is, whether there is a systematic law–preference gap for that treatment condition.


1.5.5 Price Question (Q4)
Q4 asks respondents to estimate a reasonable price for a non-exclusive license permitting all uses described in the scenario. Unlike Q1–Q3, the price variable is continuous (USD) and unbounded, which introduces the possibility of extreme outliers (e.g., respondents entering joke values or misunderstanding the scale).
The analysis applies the following approach:

Outlier trimming: The top 1% of price responses are removed per scenario to reduce the influence of extreme values while preserving most of the distribution.
Between-group comparison: Because price data is often right-skewed even after trimming, the Kruskal-Wallis H test is used rather than one-way ANOVA. This is a departure from the pre-registered plan (which specified ANOVA), motivated by the observed skewness in the collected data.
Effect size: Reported using the same VDA framework as the ordinal variables for consistency across the appendix.

title: 1  Experimental Design & Methods
text:
1.6 B1 Data Supplement
The Educational Use (EU) scenario uses a different group composition than the other six scenarios. A data quality issue was identified in the original Group B responses for the EU vignette, necessitating supplemental data collection. Ninety-seven additional participants (designated Group B1) completed only the EU scenario under the same CC Verbose treatment condition as Group B.
As a result, the EU analysis filters to Groups A, B1, and C (n = 905), while all other scenarios use Groups A, B, and C (n = 1,222). The B1 participants are included in the canonical dataset alongside the original sample; filtering is handled in _common.R (see ?sec-codebook for the full variable dictionary and ?sec-eu for the EU scenario analysis).

title: 1  Experimental Design & Methods
text:
1.7 Pre-Registration
This study was pre-registered prior to data collection. The pre-registration document is available at admin/Preregistration/THR-CC-PREREG.md in this repository.
Deviations from the pre-registered plan:

Q4 analysis method: The pre-registration specified one-way ANOVA for price comparisons. The implemented analysis uses the Kruskal-Wallis test instead, due to persistent right-skew in the price data even after trimming. This substitution is more conservative (less statistical power) but does not inflate Type I error.
Effect size reporting: The pre-registration referenced multiple effect size frameworks (partial epsilon-squared for Kruskal-Wallis, Cohen’s d for price t-tests, Funder & Ozer’s r interpretation). The appendix standardizes on Vargha & Delaney’s A across all ordinal and price comparisons for consistency and interpretive clarity.
B1 supplemental collection: Not anticipated in the pre-registration. The B1 data collection was necessitated by a post-hoc data quality concern in the original EU scenario data for Group B.

title: 4  Randomization Check
text:
4.1 Overview
Random assignment is the foundation of experimental causal inference. When participants are randomly assigned to treatment conditions, every pre-existing characteristic, observed or unknown, has an equal probability of appearing in each group. With sufficiently large samples, this probabilistic equivalence translates into actual balance: the groups become statistically indistinguishable on covariates that were not manipulated by the experimenter. The larger the sample, the more likely randomization equalizes groups across the full space of participant characteristics, including characteristics that were never measured. With n = 1,222 participants spread across three conditions (~400 per group), this section demonstrates that this experiment has sufficient power to achieve close balance on the five demographic covariates measured here.
Randomization checks verify that this equalization actually occurred and that group assignment was independent of pre-treatment participant characteristics. This Chapter reports covariate balance tests for Age, Income, Educational Attainment, Race, and Gender.
For each continuous or ordinal variable, we report a ggbetweenstats comparison plot and the corresponding omnibus test. Age and Income are tested with one-way ANOVA (parametric); Educational Attainment, an ordinal scale, is tested with the Kruskal-Wallis rank-sum test (non-parametric). For each categorical variable (Race, Gender), we report a contingency table and a Pearson chi-square test of independence.
Significance threshold: α = 0.05. A non-significant result (p > 0.05) means we fail to reject the null hypothesis that the groups are equivalent on that covariate.
While a null result is often disappointing in hypothesis testing, here it is the desired outcome: it indicates the experimental instrument is working as intended.

All five demographic covariates (age, household income, educational attainment, race, and gender) pass the balance test at α = .05. Three continuous/ordinal variables were tested with omnibus comparison tests (ANOVA for age and income; Kruskal-Wallis for educational attainment), and two categorical variables were tested with Pearson chi-square tests of independence (race and gender). None reached statistical significance, confirming that Qualtrics’s survey-flow randomizer successfully distributed participants across conditions without systematic demographic confounds.
The balance documented here supports a causal interpretation of between-group differences in the scenario analyses (Chapter 7): because the groups were demographically equivalent before treatment exposure, observed differences in infringement perceptions or licensing valuations can be attributed to the experimental manipulation (license notice format) rather than to pre-existing demographic differences between groups.
This Chapter confirms internal validity, or comparability between treatment groups. For external validity, or how the pooled sample compares to the U.S. adult population. See Chapter 5 (Sample v. Population Analysis).

title: 6  Brand Recognition
text:
6.1 Overview
Both the adoption of Creative Commons licensing and compliance with its terms depend on public recognition of the CC brand. This Chapter reports two complementary measures of CC brand awareness collected before respondents were exposed to any experimental treatment:
Together, these measures establish the baseline level of CC recognition in the sample. Because the brand questions preceded treatment exposure, they capture pre-existing awareness rather than any effect of the experimental manipulation. The results have direct implications for interpreting the scenario analyses in Chapter 7: if most respondents are unfamiliar with Creative Commons at baseline, then observed differences in infringement perceptions between treatment groups reflect the information provided in the experimental vignettes rather than prior knowledge.

title: 6  Brand Recognition
6  Brand Recognition
text:
6.7 CC Familiarity by Group
If randomization succeeded, CC familiarity should not differ systematically across the three treatment groups. The brand familiarity question precedes treatment exposure in the survey flow, so any between-group difference would indicate a pre-treatment confound — a threat to internal validity independent of the demographic balance confirmed in Chapter 4. This test parallels the covariate balance logic applied to demographics: just as age, income, and education should be balanced across groups, so too should pre-existing brand awareness.








Figure 6.3: Creative Commons brand familiarity by treatment group. The brand question precedes treatment exposure; a non-significant result confirms that pre-existing CC awareness does not confound the experimental design.











Note





CC familiarity by group interpretation. Kruskal-Wallis p = 0.166. The three treatment groups do not differ significantly in pre-existing CC familiarity, confirming that brand awareness is balanced across conditions. This result, combined with the demographic balance confirmed in Chapter 4, supports a causal interpretation of between-group differences in the scenario analyses.

The commercial magnitude of the transaction dominates respondents’ economic intuition, overriding whatever signaling effect the CC license exerts on perceptions of legal risk and wrongfulness. This pattern is consistent with the broader finding across scenarios that pricing is the question least sensitive to the treatment manipulation.

title: 7.3 Educational Use
text:
7.3.6 Summary






TipKey Findings — Educational Use



Educational Use is a divergent scenario: the depicted use — a commercial publisher reprinting a children’s book and selling 200,000 copies to schools — is infringing under the Control’s standard copyright notice but non-infringing under the treatment conditions’ CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license. This makes EU one of the study’s strongest tests of treatment effects.

Reversed ordering: A > C > B1. Instead of the pre-registered C > A > B pattern, all four ordinal questions produce A > C > B1. Treatment A respondents perceive the highest risk and want the most consequences; B1 respondents perceive the lowest risk. The abbreviated CC symbol paradoxically heightens perceived transgression rather than signaling permission.
CC symbol paradox. Respondents shown only “CC BY-NC-SA 4.0” appear to register the presence of a formal licensing regime — reinforcing the sense that the use is governed by legal rules — without absorbing the specific permissions that regime grants. The CC mark makes unauthorized commercial reproduction feel more wrongful, not less.
B1 education effect. Only B1 respondents, who read the full-text license and educational primer, show reduced risk perception consistent with the license’s actual permissions. The full-text explanation communicates permissiveness in a way the abbreviated symbol does not, but even B1 means remain above the scale midpoint across Q1–Q3.
Negative deltas — respondents want MORE consequences. All three group deltas are negative, meaning respondents believe the law should impose greater consequences than it currently does. This is the opposite direction from most scenarios. The combination of mass commercial reproduction and sale to schools triggers a punitive impulse that no amount of licensing information fully overcomes.
Null price result. No significant between-group differences in license price estimates (\(\chi^2\)(2) = 0.30, p = 0.861). Respondents anchor on the commercial scale of the use (200,000 copies), not the licensing condition — pricing is the question least sensitive to the treatment manipulation.
B1 sample size caveat. The EU scenario uses Group B1 (n = 97) rather than the original Group B (n = 414). B1 participants completed only the EU scenario, and the smaller sample size reduces statistical power for detecting effects in the B1 condition. Results involving B1 should be interpreted with this limitation in mind.

Q2 shifts from likelihood to severity: if the entrepreneur were sued, what consequences do respondents believe would follow under current law? The response scale runs from 1 (“No consequences”) to 7 (“Very negative consequences, such as incarceration and/or a large fine”), with 4 anchored at “Some negative consequences, such as a court order to stop and/or a moderate fine.” For six thousand unauthorized commercial reproductions sold at $10 each — directly undercutting the owner’s own $20 poster sales — federal copyright law provides for enhanced statutory damages of up to $150,000 per work infringed (17 U.S.C. § 504(c)(2)), making severe consequences the legally accurate answer.
Respondents perceived harsh consequences across all groups: Control (M = 5.21, SD = 1.25), Treatment A (M = 5.08, SD = 1.37), and Treatment B (M = 5.37, SD = 1.30). The Kruskal-Wallis test is significant (\(\chi^2\)(2) = 11.01, p = 0.004). The violin plots in Figure 2 show distributions concentrated at the upper end of the scale, consistent with ceiling effects. The near-universal expectation of severe consequences reflects a lay understanding that is well-aligned with the legal reality of willful, large-scale infringement — this is the kind of conduct that statutory damages regimes are designed to punish most harshly. Pairwise VDA effect sizes are reported in Table 4.

The question of whether CC-exposed groups are any more lenient toward large-scale infringers is addressed by the between-group test; the non-significant result confirms that treatment condition does not meaningfully alter normative severity judgments when the infringement is this clear-cut.

title: 7.5 Large Scale Copying
text:
7.5.6 Summary






TipKey Findings — Large Scale Copying



Large Scale Copying is the study’s most clearly infringing scenario — willful, commercial, large-scale reproduction. An entrepreneur prints six thousand posters of J. Gray’s photograph and sells them online for $10 each ($60,000 in revenue), directly undercutting the owner’s own $20 poster sales — the kind of conduct that 17 U.S.C. § 504(c)(2) targets with enhanced statutory damages.

Ceiling effects dominate. Q1 means approach the top of the 7-point scale across all groups (Control: 5.87, Treatment A: 5.71, Treatment B: 6.11), confirming that respondents universally recognize the legal risk of large-scale commercial infringement. The Kruskal-Wallis test is significant (\(\chi^2\)(2) = 24.42, p = < .001), but even where between-group differences exist, they are compressed within a narrow band near the ceiling.
Severe consequences expected and endorsed. Q2 means (Control: 5.21, Treatment A: 5.08, Treatment B: 5.37) and Q3 means (Control: 5.38, Treatment A: 5.25, Treatment B: 5.43) both cluster at the high end of the severity scale. Respondents anticipate severe consequences under current law and believe those consequences are appropriate — consistent with the legal reality that willful commercial infringement at this scale exposes the infringer to substantial statutory damages.
Minimal treatment influence. CC license exposure does not meaningfully alter perceptions for clear-cut infringement. When the conduct is this unambiguous — six thousand unauthorized reproductions sold commercially — informational framing about Creative Commons licensing has little room to shift respondents’ judgments. This confirms the pre-registered expectation that the LS scenario functions as an upper-bound complement to the Public Domain baseline.
Law–preference alignment. Some within-group Wilcoxon tests are significant, indicating partial divergence between perceived law and normative preferences. Between-group Delta: Kruskal-Wallis \(\chi^2\)(2) = 7.81, p = 0.020.
High price estimates reflect commercial scale. License price medians (Treatment A: $5,000, Treatment B: $5,000, Control: $5,000) and means (Treatment A: $19,592, Treatment B: $16,151, Control: $20,641) are among the highest in the study. The mean-median divergence reflects right-skewed distributions typical of license pricing questions, with some respondents anchoring estimates to the $60,000 revenue figure. Kruskal-Wallis \(\chi^2\)(2) = 1.14, p = 0.565.
Upper-bound baseline confirmed. Where the Public Domain scenario anchors the floor of the 7-point scale, Large Scale Copying anchors the ceiling. Together, these two scenarios bracket the range of respondent perceptions and establish that the survey instrument discriminates effectively between non-infringing and clearly infringing uses.

The Delta analysis for the Wikipedia Image Search scenario addresses two distinct questions. First, the between-group Kruskal-Wallis test asks whether CC licensing information shifts the law–preference gap: Control Delta = 0.22 (SD = 0.95), Treatment A = 0.14 (SD = 0.83), Treatment B = 0.11 (SD = 0.82). The omnibus test was not significant (\(\chi^2\)(2) = 2.59, p = 0.274). All three deltas were small and positive, indicating that respondents across all conditions perceived current law as imposing slightly stricter consequences than they personally preferred. A non-significant between-group test indicates that the licensing condition does not change the direction or magnitude of the law–preference gap — regardless of condition, respondents believe the consequences for this use should be slightly less severe than their understanding of the current law.
Second, the within-group Wilcoxon signed-rank tests ask whether each group’s Delta differs significantly from zero — that is, whether there is a genuine gap between perceived law and preferences within each condition. As reported in the Wilcoxon table above, all three groups showed significant departures from zero, confirming that the law–preference gap exists within each treatment condition individually.

title: 7.6 Wikipedia Image Search
text:
7.6.6 Summary






TipKey Findings — Wikipedia Image Search



The Wikipedia Image Search scenario is modeled on Philpot v. Media Research Center, 952 F.3d 579 (4th Cir. 2020), where a photographer’s CC-licensed images were found via search engines and used commercially without attribution. The key findings are:

Copytrap effect confirmed. The Wikipedia control — which presents the image as sourced from “The Free Encyclopedia” with a generic “Images may be subject to copyright” disclaimer — produces the lowest perceived risk (Q1: M = 3.94), lowest perceived consequences (Q2: M = 3.56), and lowest preferred consequences (Q3: M = 3.34) across all conditions. Wikipedia’s framing as a free resource appears to suppress awareness of the copyright restrictions that apply to its content.
Reversed treatment direction. Unlike most scenarios in this study, CC-exposed respondents perceived higher legal risk and higher consequences than the Wikipedia control. The general hypothesis — that CC licensing reduces perceived risk — does not hold when the control condition itself implies free availability. The CC license functions not as a permissive signal but as a salience signal, drawing attention to enforceable copyright restrictions that the Wikipedia framing obscures.
CC as salience signal, not permissive signal. Treatment B respondents, who reviewed the full license text and educational primer, rated both perceived (Q2: M = 4.11) and preferred (Q3: M = 4.00) consequences highest. Understanding what the license permits and prohibits leads respondents to judge the violation more harshly, not more leniently. The NonCommercial and NoDerivatives terms make the unauthorized nature of the promoter’s commercial use explicit.
Law–preference gap stable across conditions. Delta (Q2 - Q3) was not significantly different across groups (Kruskal-Wallis p = 0.274). All three deltas were small and positive, indicating respondents perceived current law as slightly stricter than preferred regardless of the licensing information provided. Within-group Wilcoxon signed-rank tests showed all groups’ deltas significantly differed from zero.
Philpot implications. Photographers whose CC-licensed images appear on Wikipedia face a double disadvantage: the Wikipedia context both facilitates discovery by making images prominent in search results and undermines the public’s appreciation of the copyright protections that remain. The data suggest that the general public, encountering a CC-licensed image through Wikipedia, perceives less legal risk than when the same image is presented with explicit CC licensing information.
Price estimates reflect commercial context. Median license price estimates (Control: $400, Treatment A: $500, Treatment B: $500) reflect the commercial advertising use described in the vignette. The gap between means and medians across groups indicates right-skewed distributions, with some respondents estimating license values in the thousands. Group differences were not significant (Kruskal-Wallis p = 0.090).
