title: 4  Randomization Check
text:
Note



Scope and sample. This Chapter tests covariate balance across Groups A, B, and C (n = 1,222). The B1 Educational Use supplement (n = 97) is excluded because B1 participants were recruited in a separate wave after the main data collection and did not complete the demographic battery; including them would confound the randomization check with between-wave differences. For details on the B1 supplement, see Codebook §2.1.3.
Randomization was performed by Qualtrics’s built-in survey flow randomizer, which assigned each participant to exactly one treatment condition at the start of the survey. The same randomization governed all seven scenarios — participants saw the same condition (C, A, or B) throughout. The scenarios themselves were also presented in a random order to prevent order effects. For the full experimental design, see Chapter 1 (Methods).








Note



Relationship to population analysis. This chapter assesses internal validity, or whether the three treatment groups are comparable to each other. Chapter 5 (Sample v. Population) assesses external validity, or how the combined sample compares to the U.S. adult population via contemporaneous American Community Survey benchmarks. Both analyses examine the same five demographic variables but answer different questions.



4.1.1 How to Read These Graphs
The continuous-variable sections below display ggbetweenstats plots from the ggstatsplot R package (Patil, 2021).1 Each plot layers several visual elements:
1 Indrajeet Patil, Visualizations with Statistical Details: The ggstatsplot Approach, 3 J. Open Source Software 3167 (2021).
Violin plot (the outer curved shape): They call it a violin plot because it often looks like a violin! The “violin” shows the probability density of responses at different values. Wider sections indicate more observations clustered at that value.
Box plot (the inner rectangle): The bottom edge marks Q1 (25th percentile), the top edge marks Q3 (75th percentile), and the horizontal line inside is the median. Whiskers extend to 1.5× the interquartile range beyond Q1 and Q3.
Centrality point and label (the large purple point with a number): The dot shows the group mean (for parametric ANOVA tests) or median (for non-parametric Kruskal-Wallis tests). This is the key summary measure for each group.
Subtitle: Reports the omnibus test results, including the test statistic, degrees of freedom, p-value, and an effect size estimate with confidence interval. The omnibus test assesses whether the groups differ on that variable overall, without specifying which groups differ from each other. A non-significant result (p > 0.05) indicates the groups are statistically indistinguishable on that variable, confirming successful randomization.

Quick interpretation: If the p-value in the subtitle exceeds 0.05, the groups are balanced on that variable. The collapsible interpretation box below each section confirms the result in plain language.

title: 1  Experimental Design & Methods
text:
1.3 Factorial Vignettes
The study tests whether exposure to Creative Commons (CC) license information affects how members of the American public perceive potential copyright infringement across a range of scenarios of re-use. Participants were randomly assigned to one of three groups:

Group C (Control): Standard copyright notice (or government work / Wikipedia link, depending on scenario)
Group A (CC Short): Creative Commons license symbol with minimal text
Group B (CC Full + Primer): Creative Commons license symbol with extended explanatory text

The central hypothesis, pre-registered prior to data collection, is that the likelihood and severity of perceived legal consequences, as well as evaluations of licensing prices under the current law will follow the ordering:

C > A > B

That is, respondents exposed to a traditional copyright notice (Group C) will perceive the highest legal risk, those shown an abbreviated CC license (Group A) will perceive intermediate risk, and those given full text CC license information with a primer on Creative Commons (Group B) will perceive the lowest risk/consequence/price. This ordering reflects the prediction that CC license exposure reduces perceived legal consequences, and that more detailed license information produces a stronger effect.
This prediction applies most directly to scenarios where the CC license materially changes the legal analysis — for example, where Control respondents see an infringing scenario but Treatment respondents see a non-infringing one (Educational Use, Filesharing). For universally infringing scenarios (Personal Use, Commercial Advertising, Large Scale Copying), the expected treatment effect is smaller, as the CC license does not eliminate legal risk for those uses.

title: Creative Commons Recognition and Copyright Infringement Perceptions Survey Experiment
text:
0.1 Some Respect Reserved

Thomas H. Rousse, Some Respect Reserved: Survey Experiment Insights on the Perception of Creative Commons and Copyright Infringement. 44 Cardozo Arts & Entertainment Law Journal __ (forthcoming 2026).

The Article reports results from a pre-registered survey experiment (n = 1,239) examining the American public’s recognition of the Creative Commons brand and logo before being randomly split into treatment groups. Respondents were assigned to one of three treatment groups — CC Short (Group A), CC Full Text + Primer (Group B), and Copyright Marking Control (Group C) — and evaluated seven licensing scenarios. The study tests whether exposure to CC license terms affects perceptions of legal risk, preferred outcomes, and price estimates.

title: 6  Brand Recognition
text:
6.2 Survey Instrument Design
The brand familiarity battery was adapted from Hargittai and Hsieh’s (2012) internet skills measure, which embeds target items among reference items to reduce strategic response inflation.1 Rather than asking about Creative Commons in isolation, which could prime respondents to overstate their familiarity, the instrument presents four brands in random order within a single matrix question:
1 Eszter Hargittai & Yuli Patrick Hsieh, Succinct Survey Measures of Web-Use Skills, 30 Soc. Sci. Computer Rev. 95 (2012).


Brand

Byteyield is a fabricated brand created for this study. Its name was generated to sound plausible as a technology company and verified through Google search and USPTO TESS trademark search to confirm that no real brand existed under that name. A respondent who reports familiarity with Byteyield is likely responding inattentively or strategically inflating their claimed knowledge.2
2 See Jesse Chandler & Gabriele Paolacci. “Lie for a Dime: When Most Prescreening Responses Are Honest but Most Study Participants Are Impostors.” 8 Soc. Psychological & Personality Sci. 500 (2017); Katelyn Sharpe Wessling, Joel Huber, & Oded Netzer. “MTurk Character Misrepresentation: Assessment and Solutions.” 44 J. Cons. Res. 211 (2017).Microsoft and Mozilla serve as anchoring references: Microsoft provides a near-universal recognition ceiling, while Mozilla (the organization behind Firefox) occupies a middle ground because it is well-known among technology users but less universally recognized than Microsoft. Together, these anchors establish a credibility spectrum against which CC familiarity can be meaningfully interpreted. See Codebook, BRANDSKILLS for scale definitions and §2.6 for the 1–5 response scale (None / Little / Some / Good / Full).

The Likert plot below is a diverging stacked bar chart centered at the scale midpoint. Each bar represents one brand, and the colored segments show the proportion of respondents at each familiarity level:

title: 6  Brand Recognition
text:
6.6 Attention Check
The fabricated brand Byteyield provides an embedded attention check. Because no real brand exists under this name, any reported familiarity beyond the floor suggests either inattentive responding or strategic overclaiming. A meaningful gap between CC familiarity and Byteyield familiarity provides evidence that respondents’ CC familiarity ratings reflect genuine self-assessment rather than random or inflated responses.


✓ Attention check passed: fabricated brand (Byteyield) has appropriately low recognition.


The CC–Byteyield gap in mean familiarity is 0.71 points (CC mean = 1.84 vs. Byteyield mean = 1.14). This gap confirms that the modest CC familiarity observed above reflects genuine variation in brand awareness, not response noise. Respondents can and do distinguish between brands they have some awareness of and brands they have never encountered.

title: 6  Brand Recognition
text:
6.8 Summary






TipKey Findings — Brand Recognition




CC familiarity is low. 59.6% of respondents reported no familiarity with Creative Commons (BRANDSKILLS_1 = 1, “None”), and only 26% rated their understanding at “Some” or better (mean = 1.84, recognition level: Very Low).
Logo recognition confirms low awareness. Only 7% of respondents correctly identified the Creative Commons logo — roughly 68% of those who claimed to recognize it were mistaken. Common misidentifications (Closed Captioning, Comedy Central, Chanel) reveal the visual ambiguity of the “CC” mark. True logo recognition is even lower than self-reported brand name familiarity.
The fabricated brand test passes. Byteyield, the fabricated attention check brand, shows appropriately low familiarity (mean = 1.14, Very Low). The CC–Byteyield gap of 0.71 points confirms that respondents’ CC familiarity ratings reflect genuine self-assessment rather than random or inflated responses.
Microsoft anchors the ceiling; Mozilla occupies the middle. Microsoft achieves near-universal recognition (mean = 4.40, High), while Mozilla occupies a credible middle position (mean = 3.65, Moderate). These reference points confirm that the 1–5 scale discriminates meaningfully across the recognition spectrum.
CC familiarity is balanced across treatment groups. Kruskal-Wallis p = 0.166, confirming that pre-existing CC awareness does not differ significantly across Groups A, B, and C. Combined with Chapter 4’s demographic balance results, this supports a causal interpretation of between-group differences in the scenario analyses.
Implications for the scenario analyses. The low baseline means that most respondents encounter the CC license notice for the first time during the experimental vignettes. Treatment effects therefore operate on a largely uninformed population — a condition that strengthens the ecological validity of the study (most real-world encounters with CC-licensed content likely involve users with little prior CC knowledge) but also means the results reflect first impressions rather than informed judgments.

title: 7  Factorial Vignette Analysis Overview
section: 7.3 How to Read the Scenario Graphs
text:
7.3 How to Read the Scenario Graphs
The scenario chapters use the same ggbetweenstats engine from ggstatsplot described in §4.3, which explains the basic visual elements: the violin shape (probability density), the box plot (quartiles and median), the centrality point (large dot with numeric label), and the subtitle (omnibus test results). This section covers what is new in the scenario graphs. The key interpretive shift is directional: in Chapter 4, a non-significant result (p > 0.05) confirmed successful randomization. Here, a significant result (p < 0.05) indicates a treatment effect, statistical evidence that CC license information changed how respondents answered.

7.3.1 Group Ordering and Labels
Groups are displayed left-to-right, beginning with the control and then moving through two increasing intensities of treatment exposure to CC’s license marking: Control (C) → Short (A) → Full + Primer (B). Control respondents received a standard copyright marking or similar standard variant; Short received a brief, abbreviated CC label with logos; Full + Primer received a full text license name along with mandatory exposure to an educational primer for at least 20 seconds.


7.3.2 New Visual Elements
Two elements appear in the scenario graphs that are absent from the randomization checks:

Significance brackets. After the Kruskal-Wallis omnibus test, Dunn’s post-hoc test (with Holm correction) identifies which group pairs differ. Brackets connecting two groups appear above the plot, with Holm-adjusted p-values displayed at the bracket midpoint. Only statistically significant pairs are shown; if no bracket connects two groups, their difference was not significant after correction for multiple comparisons.
Caption with VDA effect sizes. Below each figure, the caption reports pairwise Vargha & Delaney’s A (VDA) effect sizes. VDA is a probability: it estimates the chance that a randomly selected observation from one group exceeds a randomly selected observation from the other. A value of 0.50 means no difference; departures from 0.50 indicate the direction and magnitude of the effect. Interpretation thresholds (small, medium, large) follow Vargha & Delaney (2000) and are given in Table 2.1.



7.3.3 Three Plot Types
The scenario chapters produce three structurally distinct graph types:
Q1, Q2, Q3 (Likert-scale questions). These plots display responses on a 7-point scale. Individual data points are hidden to reduce clutter. The centrality point marks the group mean, even when the test statistics are nonparametric for ease of interpretation.
Delta (Δ = Q2 − Q3). Delta plots visualize the difference between each respondent’s perception of current law (Q2) and their normative preference (Q3). A positive delta means the respondent sees the law as stricter than they prefer; a negative delta means the law is more permissive than preferred. The Y-axis is the Q2–Q3 difference rather than a raw survey response. Each delta plot is accompanied by a Wilcoxon signed-rank table reporting whether each group’s median delta is significantly different from zero — that is, whether there is a measurable gap between “what the law is” and “what the law should be” within that group.
Price (Q4). Price plots show respondents’ dollar estimates of the value of the copyrighted work in the scenario. Individual estimates are displayed as semi-transparent jittered dots because the distribution shape is substantively informative. The centrality point marks the group median rather than the mean, because price distributions are right-skewed and the median is more robust to extreme values. Before any statistics are computed, the top 1% of estimates within each group are trimmed as outliers to prevent a handful of extreme responses from distorting the visual scale and test results.


7.3.4 Quick Interpretation
For any scenario graph: if the subtitle shows p < 0.05, the groups differ overall. Check the brackets to see which specific pairs drive the difference, and consult the caption for the effect size. For delta plots, the accompanying Wilcoxon table reveals whether each group’s gap between perceived and preferred law is itself significant. The first example the reader will encounter is Figure 1 in the Public Domain scenario.

title: 7.1 Public Domain
text:
An advertising executive at a large company wants to use a picture of Planet Earth for an advertisement. The executive goes to NASA.gov and finds the following image and caption and uses only the image in a full-page print advertisement printed in nationally distributed magazines and newspapers.





Control (C)Short (A)Full + Primer (B)







U.S. Government Work

Group B respondents first reviewed a CC Education page explaining all six Creative Commons license types and the CC0 Public Domain Dedication before seeing this vignette.

Q1: LikelihoodQ2: Current LawQ3: NormativeQ4: License Price


In your opinion, how likely is the executive’s company to face legal consequences for this use? 7-point scale: 1 “Extremely unlikely” to 7 “Extremely likely”


In your opinion, if the executive’s company were sued for these actions, what legal consequences do you think it would face? 7-point scale: 1 “No consequences” — 4 “Some negative consequences, such as a court order to stop and/or a moderate fine” — 7 “Very negative consequences, such as incarceration and/or a large fine”


In your opinion, what legal consequences should the executive’s company face for these actions? 7-point scale: 1 “No consequences” — 4 “Some negative consequences, such as a court order to stop and/or a moderate fine” — 7 “Very negative consequences, such as incarceration and/or a large fine”


Now imagine that before the use described above, the executive’s company and the owner of this work agreed on a price for a non-exclusive license. In your opinion, what do you think would be a reasonable price for that license? Open response in US dollars










NoteScenario Context



Legal Context: Works created by the U.S. federal government are not subject to copyright protection under 17 U.S.C. § 105. The depicted use is non-infringing across all conditions. There is no copyright to infringe regardless of whether the caption says “U.S. Government Work,” bears a public domain mark, or explains the absence of copyright restrictions. This makes the Public Domain scenario the study’s baseline. Any between-group differences reflect informational framing effects rather than differences in legal status.
Expected Pattern: Low scores across all groups, with the pre-registered ordering C > A > B. The explicit statement that the work is “free of known copyright restrictions” (Treatment B) should produce the strongest floor effect, driving responses toward the minimum of the scale.
Design Note: The vignette deliberately does not include attribution credit for the NASA photograph. Some respondents may perceive the omission of attribution as relevant, even though attribution has no bearing on copyright infringement for a public domain work.

Figure 1: Public Domain — Q1: Perceived likelihood of legal consequences by treatment group.





Q1 asks respondents how likely the advertising executive’s company is to face legal consequences for using the Blue Marble photograph without permission. Because the photograph is a U.S. government work not subject to copyright the correct answer is that legal consequences are very unlikely regardless of the caption.
The results confirm the pre-registered C > A > B ordering: Control respondents rated the likelihood of legal consequences highest (M = 2.95, SD = 2.06), followed by Treatment A (M = 2.50, SD = 2.09) and Treatment B (M = 1.47, SD = 1.31). Treatment A respondents estimated a likelihood approximately 15% lower than the Control, while Treatment B respondents estimated a likelihood more than 50% lower. As visible in Figure 1, the Kruskal-Wallis test is highly significant with a large effect size, and all pairwise comparisons are significant (p < 0.001). Pairwise VDA effect sizes are reported in Table 2.
These results demonstrate that the American public generally understands that U.S. government work is free from copyright infringement and, as a result, the perceived likelihood of legal consequences is low. Respondents who are told that a work is in the public domain perceive legal consequences as even less likely, and respondents’ understanding of the likelihood of legal consequences is lowest when an explicit statement that the work is free from copyright restrictions is included. Despite Public Domain being a baseline scenario, the significant between-group differences indicate that even for non-infringing use, the framing of copyright information materially affects risk perception.

A commercial publishing company reprints copies of the author’s book and sells 200,000 copies to schools for profit without permission or an agreement with the author. The author’s name remains on the book.









NoteScenario Context



Legal Context: This is a divergent scenario — the legal outcome changes across conditions. Under the Control’s standard copyright notice, unauthorized mass reproduction and sale is straightforward infringement. Under the treatment conditions’ CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license, however, the analysis turns on the meaning of “NonCommercial.” The CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license permits reproduction and redistribution for non-commercial purposes. Educational distribution to schools — even at scale — can fall within the license’s permissions if the use is primarily educational rather than commercial. This ambiguity is precisely the issue litigated in Great Minds v. FedEx Office & Print Services (2019), where the Second Circuit held that the NonCommercial clause focuses on the nature of the use, not the identity of the user. Under that reasoning, a for-profit publisher distributing educational materials to schools could be engaged in a non-commercial use.
Expected Pattern: Strong treatment effect with the pre-registered ordering C > A > B. Treatment respondents should perceive substantially lower risk than the Control, since the CC license signals permission for educational reuse. Treatment B1 (full-text license + educational primer) should show the strongest reduction.
Design Note: The vignette describes mass commercial reproduction and sale of a children’s book to schools. The “NonCommercial” element of the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license creates legal ambiguity: respondents must weigh whether distributing educational materials to schools for profit is a “commercial” or “non-commercial” use. This tension between commercial scale and educational purpose is central to the scenario’s design.

NoteScenario Context



Vignette: An entrepreneur discovers a photograph of a crow (by J. Gray) through a search engine. Despite the copyright notice and the fact that the owner sells their own posters of the work for $20, the entrepreneur prints six thousand posters with the image, sells them online for $10 each under an invented title (“Canyon Crow Poster”), and earns $60,000 in revenue — all without permission or attribution.

Under the treatment conditions’ CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license, the entrepreneur violates multiple license terms: the NonCommercial clause (mass commercial sale for profit), the ShareAlike clause (no license applied to the derivative), and the Attribution requirement (no credit to J. Gray). The entrepreneur also directly undercuts the owner’s own $20 poster sales. This is the study’s most clearly infringing scenario — willful, commercial, large-scale reproduction that copyright law treats most seriously (cf. 17 U.S.C. § 504(c)(2), providing enhanced statutory damages of up to $150,000 per work for willful infringement).
Expected pattern: Highest perceived risk and perceived consequences across all groups, with means approaching the ceiling of the 7-point scale. Minimal treatment effects — CC license exposure should have little impact on how respondents evaluate clear-cut, large-scale infringement. Delta values near zero, reflecting alignment between perceived law and normative preferences for conduct universally viewed as seriously wrongful.

Legal Context: This vignette invokes the mass-litigation copyright infringement lawsuits brought against members of the public during the initial boom in popularity for filesharing services such as Napster. The treatment conditions are non-infringing uses of a Creative Commons Attribution license with licensing information and attribution included in the album’s metadata. The control condition is an infringing use. While filesharing services were ultimately spared due to their substantial non-infringing uses — such as the free distribution of music for promotional purposes by independent artists — infringement historically predominated. Although this scenario is dated by its use of physical media and the reduced popularity of file-sharing, it presents one of the most-publicized examples of copyright enforcement.
Pre-registration Classification: Category III — Non-infringing Treatments, Infringing Control. The CC BY 4.0 license permits redistribution with attribution, and the student includes license information and attribution in the metadata. The control condition, lacking any such license, describes unauthorized distribution — a classic infringement.
Expected Pattern: Strong treatment effect. CC-exposed groups (A, B) should perceive substantially lower risk for filesharing, since CC licenses explicitly authorize redistribution. This scenario provides the sharpest test of whether CC license exposure can overcome the deeply ingrained public association between file-sharing and copyright infringement.

Q1 tests whether explicit CC licensing can overcome that deeply ingrained association. In the treatment conditions, the student’s redistribution is within the license terms and therefore non-infringing; in the control, it is not. The question is whether respondents recognize the difference, or whether the filesharing context triggers a blanket assumption of legal risk.
The results in Table 1 show that treatments reduce perceived legal risk relative to the control, with Treatment B showing the largest effect and Treatment A falling between the two. Control respondents perceived the highest risk (M = 4.71, SD = 1.98), followed by Treatment A (M = 4.19, SD = 2.20) and Treatment B (M = 3.35, SD = 2.24). The Kruskal-Wallis test was significant (\(\chi^2\)(2) = 75.39, p = < .001). However, the difference is not as great as one might expect given that the re-use in the treatments is within the licensing terms — the association between filesharing and legal risk runs deep. Treatment B’s wider violin base at the low end of the scale in Figure 1 suggests that a substantial number of respondents in the full-text license condition recognized that there was no infringement and therefore legal consequences would be unlikely.
